summary:
The Treaty of Verdun (August 843 AD) divided the [[event:2138|Carolingian Empire]] among the three surviving grandsons of Charlemagne — Lothair I (the eldest, who kept the imperial title and the Middle Kingdom running from the Netherlands through Burgundy and Italy), Louis the German (East Francia, roughly modern Germany), and Charles the Bald (West Francia, roughly modern France) — creating the political geography that would eventually produce the modern European nation-states. It was the first document to be written in an ancestor of the French language (the Oaths of Strasbourg, 842 AD, a preparatory document) and in an ancestor of German.

The Treaty of Verdun has been called 'the birth certificate of France and Germany' — the moment when the western and eastern halves of the Carolingian world began their separate political development toward the distinct nations they would become. The Middle Kingdom (Lotharingia) proved unstable and was repeatedly divided, with its territory becoming the perpetual borderland between France and Germany (modern Lorraine, Alsace, the Low Countries, and northern Italy) that would be contested for a thousand years. The division reflected the practical impossibility of governing a vast pre-modern empire as a single unit without robust communications and administrative infrastructure. The Carolingian ideal of a unified Christian empire survived as an aspiration (embodied in the Holy Roman Empire) even as political reality moved toward the modern nation-state system.

figures.name: Charles the Bald
figures.summary: King of West Francia from 843 to 877 and Carolingian Emperor from 875 to 877
